Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have bursts of intense focus where I'd power through readings and assignments, but then I'd drift for days without touching anything course-related. I preferred reading and writing things out, so I'd often print materials or copy notes into my own documents. My extracurriculars took up a lot of mental space, so I'd squeeze studying into whatever gaps I could find rather than having a set schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I wasn't someone who studied at the same time every day—that kind of consistency just never clicked for me. When deadlines loomed, I'd suddenly become super focused and productive, but between major due dates, I'd let things slide. It worked out in the end, but it was stressful riding those waves.
Q3: Usually a deadline or notification would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a ping about a discussion post or see that an assignment was coming up, and that would pull me back in. The gamified tasks actually helped because earning badges or seeing my progress gave me a small dopamine hit. Without those external nudges, I could easily go days without thinking about the LMS.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS did become somewhat routine, especially when I was actively participating in discussions and interacting with peers. The gamification elements made it feel less like a chore and more like something I wanted to check on. But that routine was fragile—if I got busy with other things or felt overwhelmed, the habit would break pretty quickly. It was never truly automatic for me.
Q5: The discussion forums and peer interactions were genuinely useful because I learn well by articulating my thoughts in writing and seeing how others respond. The video lectures were good too, especially when I could watch them at my own pace and take detailed notes. The gamified elements kept me coming back more than I expected—there was something satisfying about completing tasks and seeing that progress bar fill up.
Q6: I definitely ignored some of the supplementary readings and optional resources. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and then never did. Some of the longer video lectures I'd skim through or watch on double speed because I felt like I was wasting time. If I couldn't immediately see how something connected to what I needed to submit, it fell to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to my grade or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were interesting because they made even routine activities feel more rewarding. I also valued anything that let me engage with my peers because those interactions often clarified concepts better than just reading on my own.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required activities had this urgency that forced me to engage, even when I didn't feel like it. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that looks interesting' but then prioritize whatever had a deadline attached. The gamification blurred this line a bit because earning points made optional things feel more worthwhile, but I still gravitated toward requirements first.
Q9: Absolutely. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd download PDFs, print articles, and create my own condensed notes that I'd study from repeatedly. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical materials spread across my desk or while I was reviewing my handwritten summaries. I also used external resources—YouTube explanations, other textbooks—that never showed up in any LMS tracking.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours reviewing my own notes or re-reading printed materials without logging into the LMS at all. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other or discussing concepts, and none of that appeared anywhere in the system. Some of my deepest learning happened during these offline sessions where I was actively processing information without any digital footprint.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd download lecture slides and readings once, then work with those files offline for the rest of the week. I'd annotate PDFs on my tablet or print them out and highlight key passages. The LMS would only show that single download, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times before an exam.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging felt impossible, usually when my stress levels spiked from balancing extracurriculars, personal stuff, and coursework all at once. My attendance dropped significantly during those times—I just couldn't make myself show up. Even though my anxiety stayed low, I have pretty low resilience, so when things piled up, I'd shut down rather than push through.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor. When I had events or commitments for my extracurriculars, coursework became secondary. Fatigue played a role too—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to log into the LMS and watch another lecture. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, and sometimes I did, but my engagement definitely suffered during busy periods.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd be logged in, technically watching a video or scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd finish a reading and realize I hadn't absorbed a single thing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to check a box rather than actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially if they seemed tedious or if I wasn't confident about how to approach them. Even knowing something was important, I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure and put it off. It's a bad habit I'm aware of—my low resilience means I avoid things that feel overwhelming rather than tackling them head-on.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due, then drop off completely afterward. I'd cram readings, watch videos at double speed, and post in discussions all in concentrated bursts. Without deadlines, I'd drift, but when they approached, I became incredibly focused and productive.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback or good grades, I felt motivated to maintain that momentum. The gamified elements provided constant micro-feedback through points and badges, which kept me more engaged than I would have been otherwise. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose interest in that particular aspect of the course.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact. When modules were clearly organized and I could see exactly what was expected, I engaged more consistently. The gamification elements added a layer of structure that appealed to me—having clear tasks with visible rewards made the course feel more manageable. But the overall flexibility also enabled my tendency to drift and procrastinate.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they turned mundane activities into something more compelling. The peer interaction features helped too because I could learn through discussion. What made it harder was when content felt disconnected or when there were too many resources without clear guidance on what was essential versus supplementary.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is exploring, trying to understand the course structure, or feeling anxious about missing something. They might be clicking around, watching videos, reading materials, but struggling to actually produce work—maybe due to confusion, perfectionism, or just not knowing where to start. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: This actually describes me pretty well. My attendance was low and I wasn't always visible in the LMS, but I still got a distinction. I did a lot of learning offline with printed materials and my own notes. I also learned efficiently when I did engage—quality over quantity. The LMS numbers don't capture how effectively someone processes information during their limited engagement time.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling. They also miss all the offline learning, the conversations with peers, the mental processing that happens when you're not logged in. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and actively working to understand it, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement happened in bursts—when I was engaged, I was fully present and absorbing everything, but I couldn't sustain that consistently throughout the semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed materials, handwritten notes, or studying with friends. It wouldn't capture the mental effort I put into synthesizing information or the genuine interest I developed in certain topics. It also wouldn't show my emotional journey—the stress, the relief after deadlines, the satisfaction when concepts finally clicked. Numbers can't capture what learning actually feels like.
